post.content:
I'm practising for upcoming onsite interviews for senior/staff SWE backend/infra roles.

Would love to hear feedback from you guys :)

Excalidraw link: https://excalidraw.com/#json=VYhH7gPdi8_-wxco9a74W,g0U8kFS31eSiJkATrNjUSQ

![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/8d262d16-eaed-4844-8fdc-e27c25556167_1736971014.2579107.png)

References: 
* chess game: https://medium.com/@interviewready/high-level-design-for-chess-website-d037af3fc317
* top-k: https://systemdesignschool.io/problems/topk/solution

Excalidraw Libraries used:
* https://libraries.excalidraw.com/?version=2&sort=downloadsTotal&theme=light#youritjang-stick-figures
* https://libraries.excalidraw.com/?version=2&sort=downloadsTotal&theme=light#rohanp-system-design

It looks very neat. However, I don't see how it answers key questions that I would have. It might be not seeing it rathe than you.

[#1](https://leetcode.com/problems/two-sum) How are the other player and spectators be informed of a move?
Do you keep a connection open? (If you keep a connection open, what's your strategy for load balancing that?) Do you assume a data store that can listen like Firebase Firestore? Or would you create your own system?

[#2](https://leetcode.com/problems/add-two-numbers) For practical reasons, a lot of startups make really complex architectures when one beefy machine could handle it. A well-scaling system on a powerful machine can go a long way. A lot of startups have way less traffic than they desire. For example, if you have a million users, the number of concurrent games is probably much smaller. What heuristics do you have for an actual need for clustering/load balancing?

[#3](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-substring-without-repeating-characters) The matching service needs a strategy. E.g. do you run a batch job every 30 seconds and match all current people? Obviously, you can't instantly match every person because you'd have only one person to match available. Would you do a smarter algorithm? E.g. there are probably many players at the lower levels. Maybe, you can match a player as soon as another player is within 5% rank.

[#4](https://leetcode.com/problems/median-of-two-sorted-arrays) Evaluating if a move is legal requires some processing. Would you split this to two different system? One would validate the move. The next one would persist it? How do you handle loading the current board? Does the board get loaded from storage on each move? Or do you cache all active games in memory? If you cache them in memory, what heuristic would you use for memory usage? Related, how would you represent a gameboard in memory to limit memory usage?

[#5](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-substring) How do you deal with timekeeping and network hiccups? Let's say that I play with a friend. We are both on a train or subway. So the internet may sporadically be stuck, slow, or disconnected. Would you only use the server time to decide how long a player took? Or would you use a client timestamp of when the user tried to make the move? Is there a way that you could prevent hacking of the client side timestamp?

[#6](https://leetcode.com/problems/zigzag-conversion) Bot detection is probably an issue with an online chess game service.  People are going to try to use bots. They can use bots to use your UI or they can try to connect bots to your API. What strategies do you have for fending off bots?

[#7](https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-integer) What about operational concerns? How would a system update without downtime work? What could you monitor to detect issues?

[#8](https://leetcode.com/problems/string-to-integer-atoi) Kind of curious: Say you are a scrappy startup and want to keep the cloud simple and light. You could implement all the business logic in the client. The cloud simply passes messages between the two players without verifying if it's a legal move. A queue slowly writes all the moves to storage. There is no latency needed. If somehow there are illegal moves, the system assumes the client is compromised and bans the user. It could simplify the whole architecture. What would be the risk and likelihood of this running into trouble?
This design is good but overall, an overkill for a system design interview. I would focus on consolidating half of this, since you need to drill down APIs, schema design, deep dives, etc.
